caveats: a: OEWS employment and mean wages are from May 2023, while the frozen compensation input uses 2024 wages.
a: Occupation categories do not measure task time or AI exposure, and owner labor may be underrepresented.
rho: The software vendor documents available features rather than independently measured adoption or savings.
rho: Certification rules apply to restricted-use pesticides and may not cover every treatment, but they illustrate the compliance constraint on field substitution.
e: NPMA reports residential revenue rather than LMM firm eligibility, and Rollins is a large multi-brand operator rather than the target population.
e: No published count separates LMM firms by recurring versus principally one-time revenue.
s5: Rollins acquisitions include businesses of all sizes and may include locations or non-US operations; they are not a denominator-based LMM transfer rate.
s5: BizBuySell figures are across its tracked US small-business market, not pest control, and planned reasons do not equal completed qualifying transfers.
q: Public recurring-revenue mix does not measure contractual pass-through or net benefit retention.
q: The industry has meaningful residential/commercial, seasonal, and one-time revenue variation.
d5: Occupation projections cover the whole US occupation and are not restricted to LMM operators or the frozen service basket.
d5: The BLS outlook spans ten years and does not separate quantity from labor productivity.
o: Licensing and restricted-use pesticide rules vary by state and product; not every service visit entails a restricted-use pesticide.
o: Termites, wildlife, mosquitoes, rodents, and general recurring prevention may have different operator requirements.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External residential and commercial customers, primarily served through scheduled recurring agreements and repeat treatments, with some one-time and ancillary work.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive facilities units, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms whose business is principally one-time remediation, wildlife removal, product sales, or internal pest-control work.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market operators providing recurring or repeat residential, commercial, termite, rodent, insect, and related structural pest-control services to external customers.

caveats: OEWS employment and mean wages are from May 2023, while the frozen compensation input uses 2024 wages.
Occupation categories do not measure task time or AI exposure, and owner labor may be underrepresented.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion study from a representative pest-control operator, payroll-coded task data, and a technician/office survey that measures time in inspection, application, route administration, quoting, customer communications, and documentation.
a.rationale: The 2023 industry staffing table places 61.05% of employment in pest-control workers, whose core work is physical inspection, treatment selection, application, driving, and barrier creation. Office and administrative support is 17.22%, sales is 6.37%, and management is 6.29%; those functions have substantially more exposure to AI-assisted customer communication, booking, dispatch, reporting, billing, and sales preparation. The estimate also assigns a limited task share to technicians for routing, documentation, quotes, and customer follow-up rather than treating field treatment as substitutable.

d5.bridge: S2 measures US employment for the pest-control-worker occupation, not constant-price and constant-quality service quantity for the lens. It projects 5% occupation growth from 2024 to 2034; applying the half-decade compound equivalent gives approximately 1.025 for year five. Employment can overstate service-quantity demand when productivity rises and understate it when labor supply or automation constrains staffing, so the interval allows both directions.
caveats: Occupation projections cover the whole US occupation and are not restricted to LMM operators or the frozen service basket.
The BLS outlook spans ten years and does not separate quantity from labor productivity.
d5.change_evidence: BEA constant-dollar industry output, a multi-year panel of completed service visits and active accounts from major field-service platforms, or NPMA unit-volume data split by residential, commercial, termite, and geography.
d5.rationale: The occupational projection is modestly positive and is consistent with an essential repeat service rather than rapid volume elimination. The low case permits self-service, weather variation, and productivity; the high case permits invasive-pest and property-protection demand. This is a demand proxy, not a revenue forecast and not a nominal-growth estimate.

caveats: NPMA reports residential revenue rather than LMM firm eligibility, and Rollins is a large multi-brand operator rather than the target population.
No published count separates LMM firms by recurring versus principally one-time revenue.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level LMM census or broker dataset that labels recurring-revenue mix, service lines, customer concentration, and owner route participation for NAICS 561710 listings and transactions.
e.rationale: The default lens fits most LMM firms: the trade is sold externally and scheduled service dominates the public comparables. NPMA reports that recurring revenue was 85.4% of residential service revenue in 2025, and Rollins reports 75% recurring services plus 10% ancillary services. The estimate excludes firms concentrated in one-time termite treatment, wildlife, remediation, or owner-dependent incidental work.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 561710 ($8,715,666,468) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 561710 ($19,563,962,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[56]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $139,825 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 561710); class <5: 8,882 firms, avg revenue/firm $236,662 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $37,038 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 2,491 firms, avg revenue/firm $736,951 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $115,333 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,373 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,587,316 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $248,415 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 766 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,665,939 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $730,219 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 70 firms, avg revenue/firm $21,941,586 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,433,858 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 21 firms, avg revenue/firm $396,989,381 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $62,128,838 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 70

caveats: Licensing and restricted-use pesticide rules vary by state and product; not every service visit entails a restricted-use pesticide.
Termites, wildlife, mosquitoes, rodents, and general recurring prevention may have different operator requirements.
o.change_evidence: Service-line visit data showing resolution via customer self-service, remote monitoring, technician dispatch, and regulatory changes that alter who may inspect or apply treatments; customer willingness-to-pay research for professional versus DIY control.
o.rationale: Most remaining service quantity should still require an accountable operator because technicians travel to customer sites, inspect premises, identify pests, select and apply treatment, use equipment, and create barriers. Most states require worker licensing, and commercial applicators handling restricted-use pesticides must demonstrate safety, pest-management, equipment, and legal knowledge. Software can support diagnosis, records, routing, and self-service prevention, but it does not replace accountable on-site work for the majority of the current basket.

caveats: Public recurring-revenue mix does not measure contractual pass-through or net benefit retention.
The industry has meaningful residential/commercial, seasonal, and one-time revenue variation.
q.change_evidence: Customer-contract samples and renewal price-change data for LMM operators, paired with pre/post implementation margins, churn, service-frequency changes, and bid-win data by residential and commercial segment.
q.rationale: Scheduled service agreements create a credible route to retain a substantial share of an implemented gross benefit through labor productivity, denser routes, fewer manual contacts, and lower collection work. Rollins describes recurring services as ongoing prevention under scheduled agreements, states that many relationships extend over multiple years, and says customers generally sign an initial one-year contract. Retention is below full because annual renewals, competitive price comparison, negotiated commercial accounts, and service-quality expectations can transfer savings to customers.

caveats: The software vendor documents available features rather than independently measured adoption or savings.
Certification rules apply to restricted-use pesticides and may not cover every treatment, but they illustrate the compliance constraint on field substitution.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor cohort data for small pest-control customers showing active AI-feature use and realized office-hours, route-hours, technician-hours, and headcount changes; interviews with operators that adopted or rejected these tools.
rho.rationale: Scheduling, recurring appointments, route ordering, payment nudges, CRM records, reporting, and AI-assisted booking are already packaged in pest-control-specific software. Operational uptake should be meaningful within five years, but the realized labor opportunity is bounded by licensed field work, safety-sensitive treatment decisions, data cleanup, small-firm implementation capacity, and customer-service quality control.

caveats: Rollins acquisitions include businesses of all sizes and may include locations or non-US operations; they are not a denominator-based LMM transfer rate.
BizBuySell figures are across its tracked US small-business market, not pest control, and planned reasons do not equal completed qualifying transfers.
s5.change_evidence: NAICS 561710 transaction records from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, S&P Capital IQ, BizBuySell closed-deal microdata, and a confidential owner-age and succession survey of LMM pest-control firms.
s5.rationale: The estimate reflects demonstrated strategic buyer appetite but does not equate buyer acquisitions with the probability for every eligible LMM firm. Rollins completed 94 acquisitions over three years, including 26 in 2025, and says it targets profitable businesses with customer loyalty. The broader small-business sale market recorded 2,117 completed sales in Q2 2026, while retirement was the leading planned-sale reason at 45%. These facts support a material but not universal five-year control-transfer probability.

principal_driver: Recurring route-based service relationships paired with AI-addressable office, dispatch, documentation, and customer-contact work.
principal_weakness: Most industry employment is licensed, physical field treatment work, limiting direct AI substitution and making LMM transfer rates unobserved.
